topic: A person finds their spouse's secret savings account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt thick and illicit in my hand. It was tucked deep inside a rarely used drawer of Mark’s old desk, a place I usually avoided because it smelled faintly of old paper and unspoken history. Inside, nestled between tax receipts from three years ago, was a bank statement. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick thing in my chest. It wasn't a statement for our joint account; it was a ledger detailing significant, untraceable deposits made into an account with a name I didn't recognize. A slow, cold knot tightened in my stomach. I traced the numbers with a trembling finger, the ink blurring slightly under the pressure. Every carefully constructed facade I wore around Mark—the cheerful agreement, the shared laughter—suddenly felt brittle, like thin ice about to crack under the weight of this hidden reality. I stood there, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears, the air suddenly too thin to breathe normally.

[story 2]

I found the password written on a sticky note behind the spare key to the safe he kept hidden in the linen closet. My hands were slick with sweat as I typed the sequence. The screen flickered, and there it was: a balance that made my vision swim. It wasn't just money; it was a carefully curated nest egg, built in the shadows, entirely separate from the shared life we supposedly led. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I leaned back against the cool wood of the closet door, my shoulders slumping. When Mark walked in, whistling tunelessly, I kept my eyes fixed on the screen, forcing a neutral expression. "Just checking the utility bills," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally level. The forced calm felt like a performance I was failing spectacularly.

[story 3]

The discovery happened accidentally, while sorting through old financial documents during a weekend cleanout. A folder labeled 'Miscellaneous' contained a thick, embossed card. The account number stared back at me, a stark, undeniable proof of something I hadn't been privy to. A wave of sharp, bitter disappointment washed over me, so intense it made my jaw clench. I crumpled the card slightly, the paper protesting under my grip. I walked into the living room, the silence of the afternoon pressing in. I didn't confront him immediately. Instead, I poured myself a glass of water, the ice clinking loudly in the quiet space. Watching the reflection of the room in the dark glass, I felt utterly detached, observing a stranger in my own skin—a stranger who suddenly understood the vast, unbridgeable distance between what I thought we shared and the intricate, guarded world he inhabited.
